title: NOC 95100 – Labourers in mineral and metal processing | GTR Immigration
description: In 2026, as Canada solidifies its "Critical Minerals Strategy," Labourers in Mineral and Metal Processing (NOC 95100) serve as the essential foundational workforce. Operating in refineries, steel mills, and aluminum plants, these workers ensure the seamless flow of raw materials that power Canada’s industrial engine. While classified as TEER 5, this role is a strategic entry point for international workers, offering a direct path to specialized machine operation and permanent residency through localized 2026 pilot programs.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Material Handling: Transporting raw materials, finished products, and scrap throughout the plant manually or using forklifts and powered equipment.
Equipment Assistance: Feeding conveyors and crushers, opening valves, and starting pumps to assist machine operators.
Facility Maintenance: Cleaning work areas and equipment to ensure a safe and efficient production environment.
Sorting & Packaging: Sorting, stacking, bundling, and stamping finished metal or mineral products for shipment.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education may be required; a high school diploma is preferred by over 50% of Canadian employers in 2026.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided.
Physical Demands: Ability to handle materials manually (up to 20kg) and work in industrial settings (heat/noise).
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) certification is required upon hiring.
job_outlook: Retirements will create the majority of openings (approximately 70%) for labourers in this sector. As Canada expands its domestic processing of "Critical Minerals" for the global tech market, the demand for reliable material handlers in regional processing hubs is expected to remain consistent through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for labourers is heavily influenced by regional industrial projects and retirement rates:
Quebec: Good prospects in the Abitibi-Témiscamingue and Côte-Nord regions due to increased primary metal processing for EV batteries.
Saskatchewan & Alberta: Stable demand in potash and uranium processing plants and industrial manufacturing hubs.
Ontario: Moderate demand. The "Northeast Region" (Sudbury and Timmins) remains a key area for mineral processing labour.
Newfoundland and Labrador: Strong demand. High-wage opportunities are concentrated in the Labrador region due to nickel and iron ore processing expansions.
Why Work in Canada as a Processing Labourer?
Low Barrier to Entry: No formal post-secondary education is typically required, making it an accessible start for newcomers.
Career Advancement: Fast-track progression to NOC 94100 (Machine Operators) or NOC 93100 (Central Control Operators) with 1–2 years of experience.
High Retention of Benefits: Over 90% of full-time labourers in this sector receive employer-funded health and dental plans.
Strategic Pilot Pathways: Priority status in rural and sector-specific immigration pilots designed to fill "hard-to-fill" manual roles in remote areas.
pathways: Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): A premier 2026 pathway for labourers to settle in mining and processing towns with a community-backed job offer.
TR to PR Pathway (2026 Edition): A dedicated transition stream for temporary residents with 12 months of Canadian work experience.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: One of the few streams that specifically accepts TEER 5 roles for provincial nomination.
Ontario In-Demand Skills Stream: Eligible for candidates with a job offer in the primary metal manufacturing sector in specific northern regions.
